speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 27, 1996. Survivor, Sala Mydlak. Last name at birth, Fromer. Interviewer, Judy Shiffman. City, Glendale. State, Wisconsin. Country, United States of America. Language, English. Today is August 27, 1996. My name is Judy Shiffman. And I'm conducting an interview with Sala Mydlak in Glendale, Wisconsin. Please tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sala Mydlak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-Y-D-L-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-O-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 3, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And And what is your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7-- I'm going to be 72 next month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little town, Szczekocin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-C-Z-E-K-O-C-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town did you grow up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was two years old, we moved to a bigger city, much bigger, to Sosnowiec. Because my brother were much older than I was. And they had more opportunities in a bigger city. So that's why we probably moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was closer to the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell of Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-S-N-O-W-I-N-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the Jewish community of Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very nice Jewish community. In the main street, there was a lot of Jewish business. And there was-- were a-- were a lot of Jewish schools. And we even had a gymnasium, which is like university. A lot of Jewish kids attended. We had all kind of organizations I belonged. When I was 12, 13, I started to go to the organization. It was a Zionist organization. And my-- I had six brothers. And they all belonged to different organizations, one to Betar, one to Zionists, one was revisionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a large Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a nice Jewish community, lots of nice Jewish young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember kosher butchers and bakeries and bookstores?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were kosher butchers. And also, there were nice restaurants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a couple of synagogues. One large one and a few smaller ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what is your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Motl Fromer. And he was an orthodox. He-- I remember he used to go twice a day, in the morning and the evening, to the temple. And he tried to raise his sons Jewish, but somehow they rebelled. And they were not as religious like my father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about his clothing. What type of clothing did he wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the Jewish, the Hasidic, clothing he was wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have payots.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't have payots. He just had a beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear tzitzits underneath his clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wore I-- I-- I remember he wore a long, like those silky black Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: --coat. And I don't remember if he was wearing every day or just for the holidays. I can't recall that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- he had a business. He was a businessman, so a very quiet man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special memory of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was smoking a lot, all the time smoking.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cigarette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cigarettes, yeah. Made by himself, those-- I don't know what the name is of those, you know. They make-- they were making at that time their own. And he mostly, you know, he was busy. We-- we used to have a store from-- actually, not clothing, but fabric. People used to buy material to make suits and dresses. That's what kind of store he owned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zisla. Manela was the last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, her maiden name was Manela.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of person was your mother? She She was helping my father. And she was not as religious like my father. She was more looking away. She was with the children more. You know, she tried to make peace between my father and the children when they were rebelling. She was more modern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Little bit more modern, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special cherished memory of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We We love our mother very dearly. She was very understanding. Especially, I was the youngest. And I was just hanging on her. I thought I couldn't live without her. But I survived like the rest of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell Tell me about all your sisters and brothers. How many did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had six brothers and two sisters. They were much older. My mother had me already in her changes when she was over 40 when I was born. I had a married sister at that time when I was born. So there was a big-- in ages, there was a big different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names, the names of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah my oldest sister, which is alive now, she is Lola. Her-- maid-- her married name was [? Rinski, ?] Lola [? Rinski. ?] And then I had a brother Abraham, a brother Rubin, David, Moishe. There was Sal. Or, we called him Shlamek. Now, we call him Sal here. He's alive, thank god. That's Sal. And Mendek, which is survived also. He lives in Australia, Sydney. And the last one, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, me, my sister, and two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some of the things you liked to do with your sisters and brothers as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well Well, I was very close with my sister, which she was not the sister that was 20 years older than I am, because she was married when I was born. So I always thought of her like an aunt more. But I had a sister, she was only 10 years older than I am. And we are very close. She used to help me with homework, to coloring. And I was very attached to this sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle, Rochella. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You named your daughter for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my daughter's named after her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what of your brothers? Did you play games? What type of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother, the younger-- the youngest from all 6 I was also very close. He was-- he's six years older than I am. But he was-- he is-- he's a great guy. And I miss him very much. He lives so far away in Australia. Occasionally, he comes here every few years. And I was there once to see him. He was a very good son also. We all remember he was very good to my mother, to my parents. And he used to help me a lot with homework. And he's good in drawing. So we used to do those things together. We belonged to the same organization, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate all the Jewish holidays in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. We were a big family. And when it came to holidays, we all got together. And it was a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, I used to-- first, when every holiday that came, I used to cry a lot. Because I missed all that big table with all the people. And here, we're just that alone. But thank god I had my sister here. So that helped when you had a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you felt their absence.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very much. Every holiday was like a-- very sad, because I missed all of the people. And there were little kids. My brothers, two of them were married. They had babies. And I loved them so much. I was young. And I loved to play with those kids. One was nine months old when the war broke out, little boy, my brother's. And one was just maybe two months old. And everything is gone with the wind.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember spending beautiful holidays with them at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah Yeah, sure. Everybody came to our house. My mother used to work so hard. We used to help. I was still a little, so I didn't do much. But my sisters used to help her. And I really miss them. At the beginning, especially when I came here when my children were small, I was always to cry when I holiday came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember Passover. And Purim was very great in our house. You know, used to put on those clothes like here for Halloween.

speaker: interviewer
text: Costumes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to put the clothes. And-- and the neighbors used to visit each other, bring packages like [NON-ENGLISH]. So it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Hanukkah for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah we-- it wasn't as big as here. We played-- you know, we played with the dreidel. We made latkes, but wasn't as commercial like here. We didn't get so many presents like the kids today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate shabbat every week in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. Every Friday night we had the Shabbat dinner. And Shabbat was-- you know, was-- we-- we didn't-- of course, we-- you know, we didn't do much on Saturday. Because my father was religious. So we stayed home, played in the-- in the back yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue at times?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go. My father went. And he wanted my brothers to go with him. But some went, some didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any of your brothers being bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What What type of education did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I start-- seven years of school public school. Then shortly after that, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any religious instruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I had a rabbi that came to my-- a teacher that came and start teaching me Hebrew. But that wasn't for a long time. And I hardly-- I forgot. Because I didn't use it when I-- during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't too much of Jewish education. In school, we had learned the Jewish history. We had some hours Jewish history. Did you go to school with the non-- non-Jews as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you all get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember having any problems at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never experienced any anti-Semitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, later on-- not in school. But later, we noticed what was-- you know, my father was afraid to go out in the evening. And later on, they were destroying Jewish stores. They were breaking the windows. And that was like maybe it started 1935, 1936. And a lot of Jews from Germany came to our-- to Poland. And I think we had one young lady that was living with us temporary because she had to leave Germany. I-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why those people had to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of Hitler. The Jews had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were close to the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were close to the border. So we had a lot of German people coming in in our town. They had to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you train in any trade as a young girl? Did you learn sewing or any special trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. See, it was in 1938 I think I finished public school. And my sister-in-law, had a-- she had like a shop. She was sewing bras and corsets. And I thought maybe I will go. She talked me into go into her shop and start learning this trade. So I went till the-- for a short time till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You You mentioned that you enjoyed being part of a Zionist youth group or Jewish youth group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH]. And now, I have still some friends that we-- that they send me a pictures. We belong together like Girl Scouts, you know? And we used to go. And, you know I had a social life in this organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What What were your goals as a young person looking to the future? What did you want to be and do with your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really-- I didn't know yet what I want to be. I didn't think of it. I was very attached to the home and my parents and my sisters. They were so much older. And I didn't think of a future for myself at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you the summer before the war broke out in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: See in 1939, when the war broke out, we-- with-- like I told you before, I came from that little small town, from Szczekocin And I had a grandmother. And my married sister was living in this little-- in Szczekocin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from time to time, we went to visit her. And at that time, before the war broke out, a few days before the war broke out, when we went to Szczekocin just me and my parents. And then when the-- the war broke out. It was on a Friday. September 1, the war broke out. And we were in this little town. Did you know in advance that the war was going to start?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers are telling me now that they suggest that we should go to this little town and stay there. Because we noticed that something is coming. And they thought that we will be safer in this little town. There are-- they don't have factories or big houses. So that's going to be-- they won't-- they won't bombard, the Germans won't bombard so much the smaller town like the big town where I used to live. And that's-- they tell me that's the reason we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: For safety.

speaker: interviewee
text: For safety.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just turning 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, on the day the war started, did you see Germans come into-- to the town right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't Germans, because I was already-- they came to our town, I guess, first. And a couple days later, they came to Szczekocin. But we ran away. Like, Friday the war broke out. And Sunday morning, everybody was in a panic like and said, the Germans are coming. And the airplanes were flying over our heads. And we ran out from my sister's house. And there was, by the house, there was a big bus standing. And we tried to push in in the bus that we should be able to go away for the day and come back in the evening. But we never came back in the evening. Because the Germans was-- were under us. And we were on this bus, were traveling from town to town from all small towns, larger towns, for a couple weeks I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was on this bus with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of Jewish people from this small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which-- which--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was, my brother-in-law, and they had two children, my parents and I. And there were more people from the small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother wasn't there. No. She remained there. Because she was with her-- she was with her daughter. She lived there with her daughter. She-- and I didn't see what happened with them. We just were all with my sister. We went into this bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We went, because we thought we're coming back in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: So nothing, just the way we were dressed. And we had nothing with us, absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No pictures, no money, no clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not even a little jacket, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was driving this bus? There There-- he was the owner of the bus. That was his business. Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish owner, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said he would take you where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where-- we were-- there were people talking we should go close then to the-- close to the Russian border. That probably is going to be safer, close by-- by the Russian. The German won't come so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the first night, you realized you couldn't turn back.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't turn back, no. We couldn't turn her back. We just went from one town to the next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trying to stay ahead of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, trying to stay out. Because they were, I guess, behind us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Occupying.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you get food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people took us in and fed us from the towns where came in. And sometimes we slept overnight and went further. And I really don't remember everything exactly how we got the food. But I remember sleeping at somebody houses. And people were nice to us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents say to you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents explain to you at this time about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't explain nothing to me. I was old enough to understand that there is a war going on. And hopefully, it will end soon. and we'll be able to return to our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for two weeks, you traveled heading east.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, till we came close to the Russian border. That was on our holiday Rosh Hashanah. Shame We came to a small little town. I remember it was called Korzec. And we-- we came to-- we parked at a lot by a small little temple. And the Jewish people came up with the [INAUDIBLE]. They came out. And everybody took home a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was Rosh Hashanah. And my father was so upset. Because he never road-- you know, that was on such a holiday that he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Driving.

speaker: interviewee
text: --driving. But it was a war. What could you do? So they took us in. And we stayed in this small little town, Korzec. And I don't remember exactly was it a week or two. And then the Germans-- the Russians came in, marched in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Poland, yeah. And they-- the Russian, I remember, marched in with tanks. And they were throwing food and chocolate. And-- and we thought that we will go home with them, that they will go take-- take the whole Poland and we be able to go home with them. But they just occupied the half of Poland. And we stayed under occupation from the Russians. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we occupied-- it was an empty building some place. I don't remember. Was it a school or something? And from-- the people from-- maybe there were 30 of us 35-- all occupied this empty place. And we were-- we stayed there for quite a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was sustaining you there? Who was giving you food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I think there was a-- like a little black-- started like a black market. And they start like buying some liquor and taking to a different town. It was-- that's how I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how they survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit trading and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trading, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any work involved yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the Polish-Russian occupied area at this time

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in this building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe six, seven months. Then I came-- we found out that from Germany to-- it was like a delegation from Germany came into a town, which was also not too far from the town we were. And they were registrating people who wants to go back, the people that ran away. They wanted to take back. So a lot of people, they were standing in the line. And they were registrating. And a lot of them went home. I know a lot of the people that we left together on the bus, they went back home. And my father and my mom, we wanted to go back very much so. Because we wanted to be with the rest of the family. But when it came to us, they didn't-- they must have [INAUDIBLE] back farther. Like, they took only maybe 200 people or-- so I don't know if it was luck for me that I didn't go back. Because maybe I wouldn't survive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people went back to the German occupied--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we didn't go back. And it didn't take long. Then the Russian took us. And they send us away to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge when you were under this Russian occupied place? Were the communists in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Communists, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any news from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to write?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I-- I don't think-- see, when they sent us to Siberia--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before that when you were still in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Polish occupied by Russian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did no communication. No, because the war was going on already. We had no communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with the people who were on the bus with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they sent us to Siberia, no. No. We-- we separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians came in, they told you had to go to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did-- did you have a choice where you wanted to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no choice. They just came with a-- like a-- by our door. And they knocked at our door. And they said, we should go, come out. And there was like a truck. We went into-- on a truck. And they took us to the trains. And they said everybody will be there. Because I-- I-- I had my sister that was separated during that time. She was supposed to come to-- to register to go home. She came a couple of days later. And meanwhile, we were to sent out. So, and she was sent out also to Russia into a different part of Russia. And we never saw each other anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does Did she still have her children with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had two children and her husband, thank God. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she and her family were sent separate from you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a different-- different parts of Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. She was sent, I think, to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Were you taken in cattle cars or regular trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were take in cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food on this journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't think I remember. I don't-- they must have give us some food. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how long the journey took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a-- two weeks. And on the way-- can you imagine? On the way, when we stopped-- I don't know where it was-- but it was on the way going to Siberia. There was another [NON-ENGLISH]-- another-- they called them [NON-ENGLISH], a [NON-ENGLISH], like another train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw my sister and her family on the opposite side from us, you know? I wanted to get together so much with her. But they wouldn't let us. So they said, you all go be in one place. But I never saw her. [INAUDIBLE]. Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was coincidence that I saw her, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents see her as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was with my parents. And we wanted so much to get together, but was not allowed. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You would be-- were the Russians fair to you? Or, were they cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't feel any cruelty. In Siberia, we had-- like, there were-- there was like a little village, must be like an army barracks or something. And every-- two families got one little room. Was nothing in the room, except two, like, from wooden made beds. I slept with my both parents in one bed. And there is-- they put another family, a man with two sons, in the same room. And there was no kitchen, no nothing. We used to go and get-- there-- they had a big kitchen. And they gave us a card. And we used to get once a day soup and a piece of bread. I don't remember quarter of a pound, you know, of bread a day. That's how it was, you know. We were always hungry. And sometimes, we-- my mother and I, we used to go in the little villages in Siberia. The snow was so high. We used to just go in with one leg and out. And we used to dig and go for a potato or for a cabbage. Or we used to go-- later on when the spring started, we used to go to on the fields and try to find-- they already picked up the potatoes. But some of them were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were so hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because they didn't give us enough there.
